executive_summary: PIVOT verdict (68.5/100 weighted score) — strong problem validation and business model fundamentals are offset by high regulatory risk (39/100) targeting AI mental health for minors across fragmented LATAM jurisdictions, falling just short of the GO threshold.
score_reasoning:
Weighted Score: 68.5/100
Verdict: PIVOT

Score Breakdown:
- Problem: 75/100 x 0.30 = 22.5 — 16M+ adolescents with mental disorders, 70%+ treatment gap, high pain intensity with 7 paid alternatives confirmed
- Market: 68/100 x 0.25 = 17.0 — $452M LATAM mental health apps TAM, 14.6% CAGR, conservative SOM $10.6M-$21.2M
- Competitive: 62/100 x 0.15 = 9.3 — 12+ competitors but dominant incumbents (BetterHelp $950M rev); 7 market gaps identified in LATAM teen-specific AI triage
- BizModel: 79/100 x 0.20 = 15.8 — LTV/CAC 5.7x, payback 2.2 months, $29/mo subscription validated by 6 model precedents
- Risk: 39/100 x 0.10 = 3.9 — High risk from multi-jurisdiction regulatory complexity (5/25), AI provider dependency (3/25), and active litigation wave against AI mental health for minors

Knockouts: None
GO Conditions: NOT MET — weighted_score 68.5 < 70 (fails threshold); Problem 75 >= 60 (pass); Market 68 >= 45 (pass); Competitive 62 >= 45 (pass); BizModel 79 >= 45 (pass); Risk 39 < 45 (fails individual floor)
Confidence: medium — 3 of 5 departments returned status 'warning' (market, competitive, risk), and unit economics assumptions are speculative

key_strengths: Strong business model fundamentals (79/100): LTV/CAC ratio of 5.7x with 2.2-month payback period, validated by 6 comparable companies including Talkspace and Terapify — even worst-case sensitivity (price -20%) yields viable 4.56x ratio

High pain intensity with validated demand (75/100): 16M+ adolescents with diagnosed mental disorders in LATAM, 70%+ treatment gap, only 3.4 psychiatrists per 100K people, and 7 paid alternatives confirming willingness to pay up to $100/week
key_concerns: Severe regulatory risk (39/100, regulatory sub-score 5/25): Multi-jurisdiction AI + healthcare + minors regulation across LATAM with no harmonized framework — Woebot ($123M raised) and Yara AI both shut down due to regulatory barriers in this exact space; 7 wrongful death lawsuits against OpenAI for AI chatbot-related teen harm

Dominant incumbent entrenchment (competitive incumbent sub-score 3/20): BetterHelp/Teladoc ($950M segment revenue, NYSE-listed) and Talkspace ($800M+ acquisition) control the market — competing requires differentiation beyond pricing

next_steps.action: Consult with LATAM healthtech regulatory counsel in Mexico or Colombia to determine if AI triage can be classified as a wellness tool (not medical device) in at least one launch country, avoiding the SaMD regulatory pathway that killed Woebot
next_steps.rationale: Regulatory risk (5/25 sub-score) is the single biggest blocker — if AI triage requires medical device classification in all target countries, the idea is unviable as structured

next_steps.action: Run a Spanish-language landing page test targeting parents of LATAM adolescents via Instagram/TikTok ads in Mexico and Colombia, measuring waitlist signups at $5-10 CPL over 2 weeks
next_steps.rationale: Validates real demand from paying parents (not just institutional reports) and tests whether LATAM-affordable pricing ($29/mo) generates conversion — addresses the SOM estimate uncertainty (market flag 'som-is-estimate')

pivot_suggestions.direction: Pivot from AI diagnostic triage to AI-powered therapist copilot — provide AI tools to licensed LATAM mental health professionals rather than direct-to-teen AI interaction, eliminating the minor-facing AI liability and reducing regulatory classification risk
pivot_suggestions.addresses: Risk score 39/100 (regulatory 5/25, dependency 3/25) — removes AI-to-minor interaction liability, avoids medical device classification for AI triage, and eliminates wrongful death lawsuit exposure
pivot_suggestions.revalidation_idea: Plataforma B2B de copiloto IA para psicologos y psiquiatras en Latinoamerica que atienden adolescentes — automatiza notas clinicas, sugiere intervenciones basadas en evidencia, y gestiona triage de citas

pivot_suggestions.direction: Target a single-country launch in Colombia or Mexico with B2G model (government/NGO contracts) instead of multi-jurisdiction B2C — mirrors Kooth's $680M California model adapted for LATAM public health systems
pivot_suggestions.addresses: Risk score 39/100 (regulatory fragmentation across LATAM) and Competitive incumbent weakness (3/20) — B2G model sidesteps consumer competition with BetterHelp/Talkspace and simplifies regulatory compliance to one jurisdiction
pivot_suggestions.revalidation_idea: Plataforma de salud mental digital para adolescentes contratada por el gobierno de Colombia/Mexico — triage digital con supervision profesional integrada al sistema publico de salud

pivot_suggestions.direction: Remove AI generative diagnosis entirely and build a human-therapist marketplace with LATAM-affordable pricing ($15-25/session) targeting the identified gap in Spanish-language teen-specific platforms — add AI only for matching/scheduling, not clinical functions
pivot_suggestions.addresses: Risk score 39/100 (all 3 top killers involve AI regulation/liability) and Competitive gap evidence (17/20) — the 7 identified market gaps include LATAM-affordable pricing and Spanish-language teen content, which don't require AI diagnosis
pivot_suggestions.revalidation_idea: Marketplace de terapia online para adolescentes en Latinoamerica con precios accesibles ($15-25/sesion) — matching inteligente de terapeutas hispanohablantes especializados en jovenes

validation_experiments.experiment: Regulatory feasibility assessment: engage healthtech legal counsel in Mexico (COFEPRIS) and Colombia (INVIMA) to classify the AI triage component — determine if it falls under wellness, clinical decision support, or medical device regulation
validation_experiments.success_metric: At least 1 target country where AI triage can launch as wellness/non-medical-device with written legal opinion within 30 days
validation_experiments.what_it_validates: Whether the regulatory barrier (5/25 sub-score) is surmountable or structurally blocks the idea — Woebot and Yara AI failed here

validation_experiments.experiment: Landing page + waitlist test targeting LATAM parents via Instagram and TikTok ads in Spanish, offering 'triage de salud mental para tu hijo/a con IA' at $29/mo, measuring signups over 14 days with $500 ad spend
validation_experiments.success_metric: >150 waitlist signups (>$3.33 CPL) with >30% providing payment intent (email + 'notify me when available')
validation_experiments.what_it_validates: Real consumer demand and price sensitivity at $29/mo — addresses SOM estimate uncertainty and validates that parents (not just teens) will pay

validation_experiments.experiment: Interview 10-15 licensed psychologists/psychiatrists in Mexico and Colombia about willingness to supervise AI triage outputs and provide teleconsultations through the platform at $15-20/session therapist payout
validation_experiments.success_metric: >60% of interviewed professionals express willingness to participate; >40% would start within 3 months
validation_experiments.what_it_validates: Supply-side feasibility — with only 3.4 psychiatrists per 100K in LATAM, validates that professionals exist and would participate at proposed economics
